concept:
Tested 3 2006/03/09 22:33 Class of drugs that may cause
syndrome of muscle rigidity
hyperthermia
autonomic instability
extrapyramidal symptoms is: Antipsychotics (neuroleptic malignant syndrome)
Side effects of corticosteriods are:
Acute mania
Immunosuppression
Thin skin
Osteoporosis
Easy bruising
Myopathies
Treatment for DTs is: Benzodiazepines
Treatment for acetaminophen overdose is: N-acetylcysteine
Treatment for opiod overdose is: Naloxone
Treatment for benzodiazepine overdose is: Flumazenil
Treatment for iron overdose is: Deferoxamine
Treatment for neuroleptic malignant syndrome is:
Dantrolene
Bromocriptine .
Treatment for malignant hypertension (HTN) is: Nitroprusside
Treatment for Atrial Fibrulation (AF) is:
Rate control
Rhythm conversion
Anticoagulation .
Treatment for supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) is: Rate control with carotid message or other vagal stimulation
Causes of drug-induced SLE are:
INH
Penicillamine
Hydralazine
Procainamide .
A patient presents with
Macrocytic megaloblastic anemia
Neurologic symptoms indicating: B12 deficiency
A patient presents
with Macrocytic, megaloblastic anemia
without neurologic symptoms indicating: Folate deficiency
Burn patient presents with
cherry-red flushed skin
coma
SaO2 is normal
carboxyhemoglobin is elevated . What is the treatment? Treat CO poisoning with 100% oxygen or with hyperbaric oxygen if poison is severe or patient is pregnant.
Blood in the urethral meatus
High-riding prostate indicate:
Bladder rupture
Urethral injury .
Test to rule out urethral injury is: Retrograde cystourethrogram
Radiographic evidence of aortic disruption or dissection:
Widened mediastinum more than 8cm
Loss of aortic knob
Pleural cap
Tracheal deviation to the right
Depression of left main stem bronchus
Radiographic indications for surgery in patients with acute abdomen:
Free air under the diaphram
Extravasation of contrast
Severe bowel distention
Space-occupying lesion (CT)
Mesenteric occlusion (angiography)
Most common organism in burn-related infections is: Pseudomonas
Method of calculating fluid repletion in burn patients is: Parkland formula
Acceptable urine output in a trauma patient is: At least 50cc per hour
Acceptable urine output in a stable patient is: At least 30cc per hour
Cannon "a" waves indicate: Third degree heart block
Signs of neurogenic shock are:
Hypotension
Bradycardia
Signs of increased ICP (Cushing's triad) are:
Hypertension (HTN)
Bradycardia
Abnormal respirations
Decreased Cardiac Output (CO)
Decreased pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (PCWP)
Increase peripheral vascular resistance (PVR) indicate: Hypovolemic shock
Decreased Cardiac Output (CO)
Increased pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (PCWP)
Increased peripheral vascular resistance (PVR) indicate: Cardiogenic shock
Increased Cardiac Output (CO)
Decreased pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (PCWP)
Decreased peripheral vascular resistance (PVR) indicate: Septic or anaphylatic shock
Treatment for septic shock is:
Fluids
Antibiotics
Treatment for cardiogenic shock is:
Identify cause
Pressors (e.g. dobutamine)
Treatment for hypovolemic shock is:
Identify cause
Fluid or blood repletion
Treatment for anaphylactic shock is: Diphenhydramine or epinephrine 1:1000
Supportive treatment for ARDS is: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP)
Signs of an air embolism are: Patient with chest trauma who was previously stable dies
Trauma series is:
AP chest
AP Cervical-Spine
Lateral Cervical-Spine
AP pelvis
